common_name: Palace Purple Heuchera (Palace Purple Coral Bells)

planting.note: Plant container divisions or transplants in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Palace Purple is the classic dark-leafed heuchera that spawned an entire generation of dark-foliage coral bells, and remains one of the best performers in heat and humidity.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Palace Purple heuchera was the groundbreaking plant-of-the-year when it received the Perennial Plant of the Year award in 1991 — the first dark-leafed heuchera to receive widespread commercial distribution, it opened the door for the enormous wave of purple-, bronze-, and dark-foliage heucheras that followed. The large, lobed leaves emerge deep burgundy-purple in spring and hold their color best in partial shade with some direct light; in dense shade they fade toward bronze-green. Small creamy-white flowers on wiry stems appear in early summer above the foliage and attract pollinators. Palace Purple is one of the more heat-tolerant heucheras available, performing well in zone 8–9 with afternoon shade, consistent moisture, and good drainage. Divide every 3–4 years in fall or early spring when the crown heaves out of the ground; replant the vigorous offsets and discard the woody center.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Caramel shows better heat tolerance than many dark-leafed heucheras and performs reasonably well in zone 8–9 with afternoon shade and consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Caramel heuchera is one of the most warm-toned and visually distinctive Heuchera x villosa hybrids, producing large, maple-shaped leaves in a glowing amber-to-caramel color that is brightest in spring when leaves first unfurl and in fall when cooler temperatures intensify the coloring. This cultivar has H. villosa parentage that makes it significantly more heat- and humidity-tolerant than many modern heuchera hybrids — an important quality for Southern and Gulf Coast gardens where most coral bells struggle in summer. The loose mound of large, peach-to-caramel leaves acts as a bold ground-level accent that combines exceptionally well with blue-green hostas, blue-leafed ferns, and dark-colored astilbes. Small pinkish flowers appear on wiry scapes in summer. Divide every 3 years when the crown heaves, replanting the youngest outer rosettes at proper soil depth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Obsidian holds its jet-black leaf color best in partial shade — too much sun fades the leaves toward bronze, while deep shade causes them to green up.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Obsidian is widely considered the darkest-leafed heuchera in cultivation — the almost black, very glossy, rounded leaves appear nearly jet-black in partial shade, with the characteristic purple-black lobe color that named the cultivar after the volcanic glass. The high gloss surface reflects light in a way that makes the plant sparkle in shaded settings, creating an extraordinary contrast with chartreuse, gold, or bright-green companion plants. This cultivar was selected for darker color and stronger habit than Palace Purple and other early dark heucheras. Small cream-to-pinkish flowers appear on wiry scapes in early summer. In zone 8–9, afternoon shade, good drainage, and consistent moisture are essential — most heucheras struggle with humid summer heat, and Obsidian is best treated as a perennial that may need replacing after 3–4 years in warm climates rather than as the ultra-long-lived perennial it is in cooler zones.

common_name: Georgia Peach Heuchera (Georgia Peach Coral Bells)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Georgia Peach is a H. villosa hybrid and one of the most heat-tolerant coral bells available, making it a good choice for zone 8–9 gardens where most heucheras struggle through humid summers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Georgia Peach heuchera produces large, rounded, peach-to-apricot leaves with silvery overlay and a distinctive pinkish-rose tint that deepens in cool weather and brightens in warm spring sun. It is a Heuchera x villosa hybrid — the species native to humid southeastern mountains — which makes Georgia Peach significantly more heat- and humidity-tolerant than many northern-derived cultivars. This tolerance for southern summers is its defining advantage for Gulf Coast and zone 8–9 gardens. The leaves are notably large and thick with a soft texture characteristic of its villosa heritage. Pink flowers on wiry stems appear in early summer. It was selected as the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2016. Divide every 3–4 years when the crown heaves out of soil, maintaining proper planting depth when resetting divisions; a crown planted too shallowly or too deeply will decline.

common_name: Lime Rickey Heuchera (Lime Rickey Coral Bells)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Lime Rickey shows good heat tolerance and performs better than many green-leafed heucheras in the South as long as drainage is excellent and afternoon shade is provided.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Lime Rickey is a distinctive chartreuse-to-lime-green heuchera producing brilliant, highly ruffled, almost neon-yellow-green leaves that are especially vibrant in spring before summer heat causes some fading. In the garden it functions as a luminous accent that brightens dark corners of the shade border, combining beautifully with deep purple or burgundy companions such as Obsidian heuchera, dark astilbes, and black-leafed Colocasia. Small white flowers appear on fine-textured scapes in early summer. Unlike dark-leafed heucheras, which hold color best in partial shade, Lime Rickey intensifies its chartreuse coloring with a little direct morning sun and can tolerate more light than most. Good drainage is the most critical cultural requirement for heucheras in warm, humid climates — root rot from standing water or poorly drained soil is the primary cause of decline in zone 8–9.

common_name: Midnight Rose Heuchera (Midnight Rose Coral Bells)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Midnight Rose is a sport of Obsidian and shares its parent's need for well-drained soil and partial shade to maintain the characteristic black leaf color with pink spotting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Midnight Rose is a sport of Obsidian heuchera distinguished by its extraordinary foliage — deep black-purple leaves randomly spotted with vivid pink and cream flecks that make the plant appear bejeweled. The spotting is variable and unpredictable from leaf to leaf, ranging from a few small dots to large, splashed pink areas; no two leaves are identical. This speckled pattern creates an arresting, jewel-like effect in the shade garden, especially when the glossy black background reflects light from nearby sources. The pink flecking tends to be more prominent in spring and fall when temperatures are cooler and less distinct in the heat of summer. Small cream to pink flowers appear on wiry scapes in early summer. Like Obsidian, it requires perfect drainage in humid climates and benefits from division every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. An outstanding cultivar for creating focal points in dark, shadowed spots of the garden.

common_name: Delta Dawn Heuchera (Delta Dawn Coral Bells)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Delta Dawn is a Heuchera villosa hybrid with excellent heat and humidity tolerance, making it among the best heucheras for the Deep South, Gulf Coast, and zone 8–9 gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Delta Dawn heuchera is a standout among warm-climate coral bells — a Heuchera villosa hybrid that produces large, broad leaves in a striking combination of cherry-red to rose-pink overtones atop a dark purple-black base, giving an almost sunset or tie-dye appearance that changes from season to season. The H. villosa heritage provides the critical adaptation to heat and humidity that allows this cultivar to survive Gulf Coast summers where many northern-bred heucheras fail. The large, thick-textured leaves are characteristic of villosa parentage and hold up better to heavy afternoon sun and humidity than the thinner-leafed cultivars. White to cream flowers appear in summer. In zone 8–9, plant in partial shade with afternoon protection, ensure excellent drainage, and apply 2 inches of organic mulch around (but not on) the crown; the crown must remain at or slightly above soil level to prevent rot.
